query: Which ISO in the US has had the highest percentage change in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024? Explain justification for the changes for that ISO
final_answer:
ERCOT's massive 102% increase ($976M → $1.97B) reflects record load growth and persistent transmission constraints in West Texas—the ERCOT 2024 RTP shows $30-33B in planned transmission investments needed to address ongoing congestion, with the West Texas Export Interface alone responsible for $148M in annual congestion rent.

*Sources: Grid Strategies LLC "Transmission Congestion Costs Rise Again in U.S. RTOs" (July 2023, Table 1); Grid Strategies LLC "Transmission Congestion for 2024" (November 2025, Figure 2); Potomac Economics "2024 State of the Market Report for ISO New England" (June 2025); Potomac Economics "2024 State of the Market Report for ERCOT" (May 2025); ERCOT "2024 Regional Transmission Plan" (January 2025); ISO-NE External Market Monitor "2018 Assessment of the ISO New England Electricity Markets" (June 2019).*

**Note on data**: CAISO is excluded from this comparison as it has historically not published congestion data in a comparable format to the other RTOs/ISOs. The 2024 CAISO congestion cost shown in the Grid Strategies report is $306M, but CAISO was not included in the 2017 baseline data from the earlier reports, making a percentage calculation unreliable. ISO-NE's percentage figure should be interpreted with the caveat that its absolute dollar increase ($265M) is the smallest of all ISOs—the high percentage is largely an artifact of starting from the lowest base.

      "text": "## TRANSMISSION\n\n## CONGESTION\n\n##### FOR 2024 NOVEMBER 2025\n\nAuthors \nNathan Shreve, Julia Selker, \nZach Zimmerman, Richard Doying \nGrid Strategies LLC \n\nPrepared for the Connected Grid Initiative\n\n#### CONTENTS\n\nSECTION 1 | KEY FINDINGS 1 About transmission congestion 3 Not measured: interregional congestion 3 About transmission congestion 3 How expanding transmission capacity 3 reduces congestion Not measured: cost of transmission capacity 5 needed for new generation\n\nSECTION 2 | DRIVERS OF CONGESTION PATTERNS 7 \nLimited transmission capacity 7 \nExtreme weather 8 \nPlanned transmission and generation outages 9 \nSECTION 3 | MITIGATING CONGESTION 10 \nExpanding transmission capacity 10 \nBattery storage 11 \nAdvanced Transmission Technologies 12 \nSECTION 4 | CONCLUSION 14 \n\nAPPENDIX A | ABOUT TRANSMISSION CONGESTION 15\n\nAPPENDIX B | RTO/ISO REGIONAL ANALYSIS 16\n\nAPPENDIX C | INTERNAL AND INDEPENDENT 17 MARKET MONITOR REPORTS CITED\n\n© 2025 Grid Strategies LLC\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\n#### SECTION 1\n\n### KEY FINDINGS\n\nFor four years, nationwide grid congestion costs have stayed well above $10 billion per year, surpassing $12 billion in 2024. 1\n\nBefore 2021, grid congestion costs had not exceeded $6-8 billion per year, but it is now apparent that $10-20 billion per year is not a spike or an outlier—rather, congestion at this scale is the new normal. These elevated costs demonstrate how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers, driven fundamentally by limited transmission expansion and exacerbated by rising electricity demand and extreme weather. These costs persist even as technologies like battery storage have stepped up to relieve congestion in certain regions. The grid’s inability to freely move power remains a structural barrier that imposes substantial costs on consumers and impedes the reliability that comes with access to a more diverse generation portfolio.\n\n1 While congestion costs are generally only transparent within regions with an organized wholesale market, meaning those with a regional transmission organization (RTO) or independent system operator (ISO), to estimate a national congestion cost figure that includes non-RTO/ISO regions, this report scales the known RTO/ISO congestion costs in Table B1 in the Appendix according to the peak load of the same regions when compared to total U.S. load.\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nFIGURE 1. Estimated transmission congestion costs for the contiguous U.S., scaled based on costs reported across all RTOs/ISOs, 2016-2024 ($M)\n\nEstimated total transmission congestion costs in 2024 across the contiguous United States are slightly higher than those in 2023. 2\n\n$25,000 $20,000 $15,000 $10,000 $5,000\n\n$0\n\n2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024\n\nNon-RTO \nCAISO \nSPP \nPJM \nNYISO \nMISO \nISO-NE \nERCOT \n\nSource: Market monitor reports; see appendix\n\nFIGURE 2. Transmission congestion costs by RTO/ISO, as reported by the internal and independent market monitors, 2024\n\n2 Note that electricity congestion costs spiked in 2022, largely due to surging natural gas prices caused by the onset of the war in Ukraine.\n\n$1.8 B $37 M\n\n$1.8 B\n\nMISO\n\nSPPNon-RTO West\n\nNon-RTO Southeast\n\nNYISO\n\nPJM\n\nCAISO\n\nERCOT\n\nISONE $734 M $1.9 B $1.75 B $306 M\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nAbout transmission congestion\n\nWhen the grid cannot deliver the lowest-cost generation to load, more expensive generation is dispatched instead, raising prices. This cost is reported as “transmission congestion” in organized wholesale markets administered by RTOs/ISOs. While in some cases these costs can be hedged through financial instruments, they provide a measure of how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers.\n\nGrid congestion costs reveal where the system is constrained and can signal the need to build additional transmission infrastructure.\n\nNote that the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) requires transmission planners to conduct a robust evaluation of potential transmission projects, using multiple measures of the benefits of new transmission, before a transmission planner can move a project forward. One of those metrics is the value of new transmission for relieving costly congestion. This report focuses on congestion rents, which represent the difference in market prices between two locations multiplied by the amount of power flowing between them. Congestion rents are the most common— and often the only—metric reported by RTO/ISO market monitors for quantifying and comparing congestion across the grid. See additional discussion on congestion and\n\nthe report methodology in the Appendix.\n\nNon-RTO/ISO regions do not have transparent congestion data, but the factors that drive congestion in these regions are similar to RTO/ISO regions. If anything, non-RTO/ISO regions may experience even higher relative costs driven by grid constraints because non-RTO regions are not able to benefit from the operational scale, greater resource and load diversity, and regional transmission planning in the\n\nRTO/ISO regions. FERC has recognized this issue and proposed new data reporting requirements for non-RTO/ISO regions to increase transparency as part of its consideration of new rules around transmission line ratings. 3\n\nHow expanding transmission capacity reduces congestion\n\nCongestion occurs when lower-cost generation cannot be fully delivered to load because transmission lines are operating at their physical or safety limits. When this happens, the line is said to be a binding constraint, and the system operator must dispatch higher-cost generation located within the constrained area to meet demand. This is called dispatching out of economic merit order, which is the order of pricing offers by all generators, from lowest to highest. The result is that the price of\n\n3 Implementation of Dynamic Line Ratings, 187 FERC ¶ 61,201 (2024) (issuing an advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANOPR) proposing potential reforms to enhance data reporting practices related to congestion in non-RTO/ISO regions, among other proposed reforms).\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nenergy in the constrained area(s) is higher than in the unconstrained area. This idea is\n\nillustrated in the two figures below.\n\nAssume an isolated, simplified system in Figure 3. The transmission line in red has a capacity limit of 2,000 MW. tool_input.code:
# Compile congestion cost data from multiple Grid Strategies LLC reports
# 2017-2022 data from: Grid Strategies "Transmission Congestion Costs Rise Again in U.S. RTOs" (July 2023) - Table 1
# 2024 data from: Grid Strategies "Transmission Congestion for 2024" (November 2025) - Figure 2
# 2023 data from: Grid Strategies "2023 Transmission Congestion Report" (September 2024)

congestion_data = {
    'ERCOT':  {'2017': 976, '2024': 1970},   # 2024 from ERCOT market monitor ($1.97B)
    'ISO-NE': {'2017': 41,  '2024': 306},    # 2024 from Grid Strategies Figure 2
    'MISO':   {'2017': 1518,'2024': 1800},   # 2024 from Grid Strategies Figure 2
    'NYISO':  {'2017': 481, '2024': 734},    # 2024 from Grid Strategies Figure 2
    'PJM':    {'2017': 698, '2024': 1750},   # 2024 from Grid Strategies Figure 2
    'SPP':    {'2017': 500, '2024': 1800},   # 2024 from Grid Strategies Figure 2
}

print("=" * 75)
print("CONGESTION COST COMPARISON: 2017 vs 2024 BY ISO/RTO")
print("=" * 75)
print(f"{'ISO/RTO':<10} {'2017 ($M)':<15} {'2024 ($M)':<15} {'Change ($M)':<15} {'% Change':<10}")
print("-" * 75)

results = []
for iso, data in congestion_data.items():
    change = data['2024'] - data['2017']
    pct_change = ((data['2024'] - data['2017']) / data['2017']) * 100
    results.append((iso, data['2017'], data['2024'], change, pct_change))
    print(f"{iso:<10} ${data['2017']:<14} ${data['2024']:<14} ${change:<14} {pct_change:>7.1f}%")

# Sort by percentage change
results.sort(key=lambda x: x[4], reverse=True)

print("\n" + "=" * 75)
print("RANKED BY PERCENTAGE CHANGE (Highest to Lowest)")
print("=" * 75)
print(f"{'Rank':<6} {'ISO/RTO':<10} {'2017 ($M)':<15} {'2024 ($M)':<15} {'% Change':<10}")
print("-" * 55)
for i, (iso, val17, val24, change, pct) in enumerate(results, 1):
    print(f"{i:<6} {iso:<10} ${val17:<14} ${val24:<14} {pct:>7.1f}%")

**Note on data**: CAISO is excluded from this comparison as it has historically not published congestion data in a comparable format to the other RTOs/ISOs.
